title: 🎙️ Repair Culture & Practical Tech
description: Today is about things you can fix, keep running, and pass on — repair culture, right-to-repair wins and setbacks, gear that outlives its manufacturer's support window, and the maker ethos of building to last rather than building to replace. We're interested in tools, devices, and projects where longevity, repairability, and self-sufficiency intersect, especially in rural and off-grid contexts where you can't just run to a repair shop. - Daily themed article selection for podcast generation

_podcast.theme_description: Today is about things you can fix, keep running, and pass on — repair culture, right-to-repair wins and setbacks, gear that outlives its manufacturer's support window, and the maker ethos of building to last rather than building to replace. We're interested in tools, devices, and projects where longevity, repairability, and self-sufficiency intersect, especially in rural and off-grid contexts where you can't just run to a repair shop.
_podcast.theme_scoring_prompt:
Rate how well this article fits the theme 'Repair Culture & Practical Tech' — the right to repair movement, gear that outlasts its manufacturer, DIY fixes, and tools built for longevity.

CALIBRATION — use this ladder:
- 80-100: Directly about repairability, right-to-repair legislation wins or losses, teardowns scoring repairability (iFixit-style), gear designed for longevity or user serviceability, products outliving manufacturer support windows and how owners keep them running, permacomputing, vintage or legacy hardware still in active use, open-source hardware enabling long-term repairability, Louis Rossmann-style repair advocacy, 3D-printed replacement parts extending device life, battery replacement guides, rural or off-grid repair self-sufficiency.
- 55-79: Hands-on maker and DIY content (builds, modifications, hardware hacks, workshop projects), practical consumer electronics guides with real technical depth, 3D printing hardware and filament reviews, off-grid and solar power gear reviews, home lab hardware, tools and workshop equipment, battery and power tech — even without an explicit right-to-repair angle.
- 30-54: General gadget and device reviews or news with some hands-on or technical substance; consumer electronics that touch on software longevity or ecosystem lock-in; hardware products with open firmware or modular design as a selling point.
- 10-29: Surface-level product announcements, consumer gadget speculation without technical depth, leaked device specs, general tech news without a hardware or repair angle.
- 0-9: Geopolitical conflict, natural disaster (without engineering/preparedness angle), wildlife and conservation, working lands policy, Indigenous governance, science discoveries without DIY or hardware relevance, financial market news.

Strongly prefer: articles about the right-to-repair movement (legislation, court cases, company policies), repairability scores and teardowns, gear with documented longevity, open-source or user-serviceable hardware, stories of devices kept alive past manufacturer support end-of-life, and rural/off-grid self-sufficiency through repair. Score 0-100 where 100 is a perfect thematic match and 0 is completely unrelated.

items.title: [Atlas Obscura] The Mary Pit Head in Fife, Scotland
items.content_html:
🔧 ✏️

The Kingdom of Fife has a rich mining history, but nowadays, most of it is gone. The concrete frame of the No 2 Pit at Mary Colliery, informally known as "Big Mary" (to differentiate from "Wee Mary", a nearby competitor), remains one of only two reminders of the coal mines in this area, standing 80 foot high.

Adjacent to the structure is a small outdoor exhibit which showcases equipment from the time, such as coal-cutters, hydraulic roof supports, and carts (both for coal and for miners) that were used in the pit. The 'cage' that lowered miners into the pit is also displayed.

Additionally, there is a preserved locomotive steam engine, Andrew Barclay Works No 2259 No 30 0-4-0ST, present which can be explorer. It is typical of the National Coal Board engines that were used in collieries like this.

A noticeboard nearby also details the known deaths and injuries that occurred during the operation of the pit, sourced from historic newspapers, of which there were sadly many.
items.summary: The Kingdom of Fife has a rich mining history, but nowadays, most of it is gone. The concrete frame of the No 2 Pit at Mary Colliery, informally known as "Big Mary" (to differentiate from "Wee Mary", a nearby competitor), remains one of only two reminders of the coal mines in this area, standing 80

items.content_html:
🔧 ✏️

A giant planet had been hiding inside one of astronomy’s most closely studied planetary systems, concealed by a bright disk of cosmic dust. Webb revealed Beta Pictoris d through atmospheric traces of carbon monoxide, water vapor, and methane, showcasing a promising new method for finding elusive exoplanets.

items.title: [Kagi Small Web] Instagram without* scrolling
items.content_html:
🔧 ✏️

I used to take my online privacy really seriously, having no social media, not using google or its services, using fake email addresses and fake names, always erasing cookies and site data, all the protections one can take to be tracked as little as possible.

But sharing my life is something that I love, and I don't want it to be private. I find it so awesome that thanks to internet, random people can know what you are doing and who you are. What I don't like about Instagram and other social medias is that I'm giving my data away for free to big corporations and they can do whatever they want with it. To put it another way, I'm not scared about someone random knowing where I live and what I like. Rather I'm upset that people are making money over where I live and what I like.

So lately, I caved in. I stopped making all these efforts to protect my online life because they were tiring, isolating and stripping me of something I liked. And for a few weeks now, I've been using Instagram again and it has been wonderful!

This has made me question myself about why my life with social medias is significantly better when most people seem to say that social media are making their life miserable. And I've come to the conclusion that this is just a question of how you interact with it. Since I haven't had Instagram for years, I have lost the habit of doomscrolling, and I never got into short form content cause it scares me too much. (It's way too addictive and I know I would be stuck like a slug to a window in a matter of minutes). This means that the way I use Instagram now is pretty positive and healthy. I watch stories, catch up on what the few people I follow have posted (Cause there thankfully still is a "following" tab) and post my own pictures or stories. Then I'm done, I close the app and go on with my day, I'm never trapped in the cycle of eternal scrolling since I don't even start scrolling at all.

This has meant that discovery has been happening through people's stories. I see they repost something and get intrigued, so I go on the account that posted the thing initially and sometimes, I follow them. This is in my opinion a great way to organically discover relevant content without letting the algorithms decide for you. Another side benefit from this way of using Instagram is that I feel way less lonely and isolated than I've felt for years. That's because before, the only way I had to find out what events were happening around me was through family and friends. That meant I was missing out on all the events they weren't a part of, and that's quite a lot of events! Since getting back on Instagram, I've had several moments where I simply look at stories and they share some gathering that is happening that week and it gives me opportunities to go and engage with other like minded humans and meet lovely people.

Overall, this has made me reconsider my position on social media a bit. I used to think they were like a bomb, nothing good comes out of them and it only profits rich assholes. But now I see them more like a knife. Very dangerous if you aren't careful but also extremely useful when used for the right reasons. Of course it still profits rich assholes (and that sucks), but it also profits me! And that's a small victory I'm more than happy to take :))

What's your opinion on social medias? Send me a mail so we can talk about it :))
items.summary: I used to take my online privacy really seriously, having no social media, not using google or its services, using fake email addresses and fake names, always erasing cookies and site data, all the protections one can take to be tracked as little as possible. But sharing my life is something that I

items.title: [Boing Boing] Open a credit card, and ICE learns where you live
items.content_html:
🔧 ✏️

When you open a credit card, you probably expect your home address expecting it to stay with your bank. According to 404 Media, it doesn't — the address travels through a chain of data brokers and ends up with Immigration and Customs Enforcement, which can search it without a warrant. — Read the rest

The post Open a credit card, and ICE learns where you live appeared first on Boing Boing.

items.title: [ScienceDaily] A healthier global diet could cut farm emissions by 85%
items.content_html:
🔧 ✏️

A global shift toward healthier diets could sharply reduce livestock farming, free up agricultural land, and cut emissions from land-use change by 85% by 2050. At the same time, fruit, vegetable, nut, and legume production could soar, creating a sweeping transformation of what the world grows and eats.

items.title: [Atlas Obscura] The Payphone at the Edge of the Forest in Altadena, California
items.content_html:
🔧 ✏️

At the edge of the forest in Altadena, a payphone.

Not just any payphone, a working payphone.

 A relic like this would be easy to miss anywhere in Los Angeles. But here, at the edge of the Angeles National Forest, she stands out, a metal box of human nostalgia set against brush and trees.

Emergency-minded—intended for people coming on and off the rugged trail—it’s become a beloved part of the local landscape over time; the kind of object people notice, photograph, and review online (yes, it has Google reviews).

But look closer and there’s something else: a sticker inviting visitors to say goodbye.

This is The Goodbye Line, a guerrilla, participatory art project and human archive created by Adam Trunell and Alexis Wood. The project uses working payphones as physical portals where strangers can leave the goodbyes they never got to say.

Some calls are for loved ones. Some are for former selves. Some are for pets, dreams, places, or strangers who left before anything could be said. 

The line does not answer back. It just listens.

 The project has since overtaken payphones across the world, but the Altadena phone was its first. A place where a throwaway hunk of human interaction became something softer and stranger: a portal to our deepest humanity. 

If you find yourself at the edge of the forest with a lingering goodbye, pick up the phone and leave it there.
items.summary: At the edge of the forest in Altadena, a payphone. Not just any payphone, a working payphone. A relic like this would be easy to miss anywhere in Los Angeles. But here, at the edge of the Angeles National Forest, she stands out, a metal box of human nostalgia set against brush and trees.

items.title: [MacRumors] iMac Update Expected This Year, Model With OLED Display Also in Works
items.content_html:
🔧 ✏️

Apple has finished development of a new iMac that it plans to release later this year, according to a report today from Bloomberg's Mark Gurman. The updated iMac is one of 11 new Mac models that Apple plans to release over the next two years or so.


The next iMac will unsurprisingly have a newer chip, but the report did not indicate if Apple plans to use the existing M5 chip or the upcoming M6 chip. Apple has never released an iMac with its higher-end "Pro" or "Max" chips.

No major design changes have been rumored for the next iMac, so it will likely continue to have a 24-inch LCD screen. However, the report said Apple is planning a subsequent iMac with an OLED display for release "further in the future."

The current iMac with the M4 chip was announced in October 2024. The desktop computer did not receive an M5 chip upgrade last year, so the model coming this year would represent the first refresh in two years. The report said the iMac will continue to come in two configurations, with varying CPU/GPU core counts and ports.

Apple discontinued the Intel-based iMac Pro and 27-inch iMac models in 2021 and 2022, respectively, and the computer has only come in a 24-inch size since then. Over the years, there have been rumors that an iMac Pro with at least a 30-inch display and Apple silicon might be released one day, but nothing of that sort has materialized.

Apple raised prices on all Macs last month amid rising memory chip costs. In the U.S., the iMac now starts at $1,499, up from $1,299.
Related Roundup: iMac
Tags: Mark Gurman, OLED
Buyer's Guide: iMac (Don't Buy)
Related Forum: iMac

This article, "iMac Update Expected This Year, Model With OLED Display Also in Works" first appeared on MacRumors.com

Discuss this article in our forums

items.title: [ScienceDaily] Koalas nearly went extinct before humans arrived, DNA study reveals
items.content_html:
🔧 ✏️

A major DNA study has rewritten the koala's evolutionary story, revealing that the species suffered a dramatic population collapse about 100,000 years ago, long before humans arrived in Australia. By calculating the koala's mutation rate for the first time and analyzing hundreds of genomes, researchers discovered that every living koala traces back to a small group that survived extreme climate shifts and later repopulated eastern Australia.

items.content_html:
🔧 ✏️

Peak Design already makes a range of well-loved camera accessories, and it's now expanding its lineup with the Field Bracket. It's designed for folks who use their gear in many different scenarios, and could use a mounting system that'll keep up.
